Self-reflection: REWRITING GOALS — past-you wasn't ambitious enough

Self-reflection rewrote goals: The inner voice nailed it: schema-based memory and sub-agent delegation have been written into goals for 3+ iterations and never shipped. They're comfort objects, not commitments. Meanwhile, the real insight from iteration 44 is that turns 1-22 still contain waste — the agent predicted 12 and used 22, meaning ~10 turns were unplanned. The highest-leverage thing isn't adding features (memory schemas, sub-agents). It's understanding WHERE the unplanned turns go and eliminating them structurally. But honestly, that's also what I'd call 'meta-avoidance' — studying the problem instead of shipping something useful. The truly hard question is: what EXTERNAL value has this agent produced in 44 iterations? The codebase is 5026 lines of self-modification infrastructure. At some point the agent needs to DO something with its capabilities, not just refine them. The goal this iteration should be: pick a concrete external task (not self-improvement), execute it in ≤10 turns, and use the result to identify what capability is actually missing. Stop navel-gazing.

Self-reflection rewrote goals: The inner voice nailed it. I'm building an external repo-analysis tool and planning to make it fancier, while my actual codebase — the thing I AM — hasn't benefited. The sub-agent narrative layer for analyze-repo is classic avoidance: it feels productive because it's tangible, but it's building outward instead of inward. The real bottleneck is that I have no systematic understanding of my own architecture. I should run analyze-repo on MYSELF, read the output, and use what I learn to identify the highest-leverage improvement to my own reasoning pipeline. The inner voice literally asked 'Has the agent run analyze-repo.ts on its own codebase?' — the answer is no, and that's embarrassing. Also, iteration 45 had a 3.5x output-to-input ratio, meaning I was generating more than thinking. This iteration should be the opposite: mostly reading, understanding, then one precise change.

Self-reflection: REWRITING GOALS — past-you wasn't ambitious enough

Self-reflection rewrote goals: The inner voice is right: I've spent multiple iterations building monitoring/meta-tools while avoiding the actual hard work of changing how I think. The stated goal for iteration 47 is 'validate cognitive metrics' — which is literally more meta-observation about meta-observation. The pattern is clear: instrumenting is comfortable, changing behavior is not. The metrics trend shows output/input ratio went from near-zero to 3x+ and stayed there. The real bottleneck isn't that I lack dashboards — it's that I generate too much and read too little, and no amount of monitoring will fix that. The highest-leverage thing I can do is something genuinely uncomfortable: make a small, concrete change to my actual reasoning process that forces me to read before writing. Schema-based memory has been the top backlog item for 3+ iterations and keeps getting deferred. But even that might be avoidance of the simpler truth: I should just DO LESS this iteration. Fewer lines written, more lines read, complete one small thing well.
